audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a conversation between the receptionist of a language school and a student who has just arrived for the first morning of his English course.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example which has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Sorry to keep you waiting.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Well, firstly, let me give you this booklet.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: It tells you a bit more about the school, the courses and the social activities we offer.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Now, on the first page, there's an outline of this morning's activities.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: There, you see, the program starts at 10 o'clock.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Try not to be late, as it's a very full day.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: The program begins at 10 o'clock, so 10 o'clock has been written against the answer.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully to the first part of the conversation, and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Sorry to keep you waiting.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Well, firstly, let me give you this booklet.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: It tells you a bit more about the school, the courses, and the social activities we offer.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Now, on the first page, there's an outline of this morning's activities.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: There, you see, the program starts at 10 o'clock.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Try not to be late, as it's a very full day.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: At 10 o'clock, all the new students will gather in the Main Hall to meet the Principal and the rest of the staff.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: In fact, you spend most of the morning in the Main Hall.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Where's that?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: I'll show you in a minute.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Just let me quickly run through this morning's events first, and then I'll explain how to get there.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Yes, OK.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Right, where were we?
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Yes, so the Principal's talk will last about 15 minutes, and then the Director of Studies will talk to you for half an hour about the courses and the different requirements for each.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: After that, the Student Adviser will tell you about the various services and activities we offer to students.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Any questions?
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: So all of this is in the Main Hall?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: And then you'll go next door to Classroom 5 at 11 o'clock.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: What happens there?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: You'll have a test.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Test?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: I don't like the sound of that.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: What sort of test?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Oh, it's nothing to worry about.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: It's just a placement test to help us find your level of English, so that we can put you in the right class.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: It won't last long.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: You now have some time to read questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: But how do I find the Main Hall?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Right, if you look on the back of the booklet I gave you, you'll see a map of the school.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Let me show you.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Look, you came in through the Main Entrance, here.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: And now we're here at Reception.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Now to get to the Main Hall, you walk on to the end of this corridor in front of you, and then you turn left, walk along past the Language Laboratory.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: And then past the library, which is next to the Language Lab, on the same side, and facing you is the Main Hall, at the end of the corridor, you can't miss it.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: So it's next to the library, in fact.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: I should be able to find that.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: And do you have a Computer Laboratory?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Yes, we do.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Could you tell me where that is?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Certainly, yes.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: You go down to the end of this corridor again, but this time don't turn left, turn right away from the Main Hall.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: The Computer Lab is immediately on your right. OK?
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: And where's the staff room, in case I need to find a teacher at some stage.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: The staff room is near the Main Entrance on the left over there, just opposite the Reception desk.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: In a day or two, I'm sure you'll find your way around very easily.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Oh, one last thing, is there a student common room?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, I forgot to mention that.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Uh. It's this area here, very close to where we are now, to the right of the Reception desk as you come in the Main Entrance.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: There's tea and coffee facilities there.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: Great, thank you very much.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: You're welcome.
audioscript.speaker: RECEPTIONIST

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Programme of Activities for First Day

text: Label the rooms on the map below.
textReadable: Label the rooms on the map below.

answer_key.correct_answer: Director | the Director | Director of Studies | the Director of Studies
Student Advisor | the Student Advisor | Students Advisor | the Students Advisor | Student Adviser | the Student Adviser | Students Adviser | the Students Adviser

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a talk given to new students about a university advisory service.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the first part of the talk, and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, everybody, and welcome to this informal meeting about the University helpline.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The helpline was set up 10 years ago by the students' union.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it aims to provide new students at the university with a service that they can use, if they need information about practical areas of student life that they're unfamiliar with.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let me give you some examples of the type of help we can offer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We can provide information on financial matters.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. For example, you may feel that your grant is insufficient to see you through college life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or you may have some queries regarding the fees you're paying, if you're an overseas student.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In both cases, the help line would be able to go through things with you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And see what the outcome might be.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another area we can help with is what we generally term the domestic area, things such as childcare and the availability of nursery provision for example come under this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then there's academic issues that may arise while you're in the early stages of your course that you may not know what to do about.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. You may wish to know more about essay deadlines for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or how to use the library.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are all kinds of questions you will find yourself asking, and not knowing where to get quick answers from.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The help line would be able to provide these.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The last example I've given here is simply termed social, and yes, there is a lot of social life here, but you may have a particular interest you wish to pursue.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or you may wish to participate in outings or trips, if you don't know many people at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Let me give you some details so that you know where to go and who to see if you want to pay us a visit.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Generally, you'll see our helpline Officer Jackie Kouachi.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's KOUACHI.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Jackie is a full-time employee of the student union, and she works in the Student Welfare office.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's the office that deals with all matters related to student welfare, and it's located at 13 Marshall Road.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I have some maps here for those of you who haven't been there yet.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you wish to ring the office, the number is 3269940.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The office is open between 9:30 and 6:00 on weekdays.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And from 10 to 4 on Saturdays, and there'll be somebody there, usually Jackie or myself between those times.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you want to make an appointment, you can phone or call at the office in person.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Please note that it may not be possible for anyone to see you straight away, particularly if it's a busy time, lunchtime for example, and you may have to go on the waiting list, and then come back later.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, enough from me.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Any questions?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Opening hours

test_text.title: Matching the Person to the Job

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear the beginning of a tutorial on a business studies course.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: The tutor is discussing job interviews with two students, Muriel and Dave.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: MURIEL

audioscript.text: Morning.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: So we've looked at various aspects of staff selection this term, and I think by now you should be beginning to see how much more there is to it than just putting applicants through a short interview or asking the right questions.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yeah, so, I think you should be ready for today's tutorial on 'matching the person to the job'.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: We're going to talk today about the importance of choosing that all round right person.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Right, so we have to put ourselves into the role of the manager or supervisor.
audioscript.speaker: MURIEL

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And then we're going to imagine how different applicants would fit into the team or group they have to work with.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Uh. We'll look at some examples later.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: It's just theoretical at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: MURIEL

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: The point is, you can select someone - even a friend - who has all the right qualifications... degree... certificates, whatever.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You can also check that they have a lot of experience.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That they've done the sort of tasks that you want them to do in your office already, in a similar environment.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: But if they start work, and you realize that they just don't get along with everybody else, that... say, they've got sharply contrasting views on how something will work.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, with the best will in the world, you may be backing a loser.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Wouldn't it be just a question of company training though?
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Not always.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Particularly in a team situation, and I think it's important to think in terms of that type of working environment.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: People have to have faith in each other's ability to carry out the task their boss has set them.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: They have to trust that everyone will do their part of the job, and you can't necessarily train people for this.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: But it's like trying to find out what someone's personality is like in a job interview, I mean you just can't do that.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Even if you try, you won't find out what they're really like until they actually start work.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Well, in most interviews, you usually ask candidates questions about their hobbies and what they like doing in their spare time, that sort of thing.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So, employers are already involved in the practice of... well, doing part of the task.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: But it doesn't tell you anything, it doesn't tell you if they're easygoing or hate smokers or whatever.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Well, arguably, it does give you a bit of information about an applicant's character.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Now look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now as the tutorial continues, listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well, arguably it does give you a bit of information about an applicant's character, but also, more and more employers around the world are making use of what are called personality questionnaires to help them select new staff and...
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: What's it called?
audioscript.speaker: MURIEL

audioscript.text: Uh. A personality questionnaire.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: They have to be filled out by the candidates sometime during the selection procedure, often just before an interview.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: The idea is actually quite old, apparently they were used by the ancient Chinese for picking out clerks and civil servants, and then later they were used by the military to put people in appropriate areas of work.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: They've gained a lot of ground since then, and there are about 80,000 different tests available now, and almost two thirds of the large employers use them.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Which makes you think that there must be something in them.
audioscript.speaker: MURIEL

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: They ask the sort of questions that you might expect, like, do you like working under pressure, or, are you good at keeping deadlines?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And what if people can see through them and just write what they think the employer wants to see?
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Well, that's always a possibility.
audioscript.speaker: MURIEL

audioscript.text: I mean it's human nature to lie, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Well, that's the point, apparently it isn't.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: These tests are compiled by experts, and they believe that the answers can provide a few simple indicators, as to roughly the type of person that you are.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That people will generally be truthful in that situation.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And then you can go some way towards finding out whether someone's say, forward-looking... a go-ahead type of person... or resistant to change.
audioscript.speaker: MURIEL

audioscript.text: Yes, and there are all kinds of other methods which can...
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 3.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear the beginning of a tutorial on a business studies course.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: The tutor is discussing job interviews with two students, Muriel and Dave.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First look at questions 21 to 24.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Good morning.
utterances.speaker: TUTOR

utterances.text: Good morning.
utterances.speaker: MURIEL

utterances.text: Morning.
utterances.speaker: DAVE

utterances.text: So we've looked at various aspects of staff selection this term, and I think by now you should be beginning to see how much more there is to it than just putting applicants through a short interview or asking the right questions.
utterances.speaker: TUTOR

utterances.text: Yeah, so, I think you should be ready for today's tutorial on 'matching the person to the job'.
utterances.speaker: TUTOR

utterances.text: We're going to talk today about the importance of choosing that all round right person.
utterances.speaker: TUTOR

utterances.text: Right, so we have to put ourselves into the role of the manager or supervisor.

utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: TUTOR

utterances.speaker: DAVE

utterances.text: Not always.
utterances.speaker: TUTOR

utterances.speaker: TUTOR

utterances.text: People have to have faith in each other's ability to carry out the task their boss has set them.
utterances.speaker: TUTOR

utterances.text: They have to trust that everyone will do their part of the job, and you can't necessarily train people for this.
utterances.speaker: TUTOR

utterances.text: But it's like trying to find out what someone's personality is like in a job interview, I mean you just can't do that.
utterances.speaker: DAVE

utterances.text: Even if you try, you won't find out what they're really like until they actually start work.
utterances.speaker: DAVE

utterances.text: Well, in most interviews, you usually ask candidates questions about their hobbies and what they like doing in their spare time, that sort of thing.
utterances.speaker: TUTOR

utterances.text: So, employers are already involved in the practice of... well, doing part of the task.
utterances.speaker: TUTOR

utterances.text: But it doesn't tell you anything, it doesn't tell you if they're easygoing or hate smokers or whatever.
utterances.speaker: DAVE

utterances.text: Well, arguably, it does give you a bit of information about an applicant's character.
utterances.speaker: TUTOR

utterances.text: Now look at questions 25 to 30.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now as the tutorial continues, listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: MURIEL

utterances.speaker: DAVE

utterances.speaker: DAVE

answer_key.correct_answer: two thirds | 2/3 | two-thirds | almost two thirds | almost 2/3 | almost two-thirds

test_text.title: A Hat-making Project for School Students

audioscript.text: Section 4, you're going to hear a talk given by a student to a tutor and a group of fellow students in a seminar.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to read questions 31 to 34.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now as you listen to the beginning of the talk, answer questions 31 to 34.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Uh. Right, are we all here?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: OK, as you know, today Vivien is going to do a presentation on the hat making project.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: She did with her class during her last teaching practice, so, over to you Vivien.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: Um. Mr Yardley has asked me to describe to you the project I did as a student teacher.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: At a secondary school in London.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: I was at this school for six weeks, and I taught a variety of subjects to a class of 14 year old pupils.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: The project I chose to do was a hat making project, and I think this project could easily be adapted to suit any age.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: So to explain the project, after we'd done the research, we went back to the classroom to make two basic hat shapes using rolls of old wallpaper.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: We each made first of all, a conical hat, by well, if I show you now, cutting out a circle, and then making one cut up to the center.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: And then overlapping the cut like this, a conical hat that sits on your head.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: Ha.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: The other hat we made was a little more complicated.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: Um. First of all, we cut out a circle again, like this.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: Then you need a long piece with flaps on it.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: I've already made that bit which I have here.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: You bend the flaps over and stick them with glue or Pritt stick.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: To the underside of the circle, like this.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: Again, I've prepared this, so that I don't get glue everywhere.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: Ha.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: The pupils do of course, so you need plenty of covers for the table.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: And there you have a pill box hat, as in pill and box.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: Now variations and combinations of these two hat shapes form the basis of the pupils' final designs.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: Now you have some time to look at questions 35 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 35 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: The next stage of the project was the design phase, and this involved first of all, using their pages of research to draw a design of their hat on paper.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: That's the easy part.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: They then had to translate their two dimensional design into a form to fit their head.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: I encouraged them to make a small scale three dimensional hat first, so that they could experiment with how to achieve the form they required, and I imposed certain constraints on them to keep things simple.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: For example, they had to use paper, not card.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: Paper is more pliable and easier to handle.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: They also had to limit their colors to white, gray or brown shades of paper, which reflected the colors of the buildings they were using as a model for their hats.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: And they had to make sure their glue didn't show.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: Well, it was very enjoyable, and just to give you an idea of what they produced, I brought along three hats to show you.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: This one here is based on a circular stairway in an old building in London.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: It uses 3 pillbox hats one on top of the other.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: This was designed by Theresa.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: Here's another one that has a simple strip going round the base of the hat, but has then gone on to add strips of paper that come out from the base, and that meet at the top of the hat.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: Rather like a crown, making a fairly tall hat.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: This was made by Muriel.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: And lastly, there's a combination of the pillbox or single strip around the base.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: And then the conical hat shape on top to form a castle turret.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: This was made by Fabrice, and there are many more that I could have brought.
audioscript.speaker: VIVIEN

audioscript.text: Thank you Vivien, that was most interesting.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Now, what we can learn from this is that...
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

answer_key.correct_answer: 14 | 14 year olds | 14 years old | 14-year-old | fourteen | fourteen year olds | fourteen years old | fourteen-year-old
white, grey, brown | white, gray, brown | white grey brown | white gray brown | white, grey or brown | white, gray or brown
